title: Year Three of the Kāne'ohe Bay Coral Resilience Study: What the Data Tells Us
content: Three years into our long-term monitoring transects, the picture emerging from Kāne'ohe Bay is both hopeful and sobering. Coral cover on the northern patch reefs has recovered an average of 11 percent since our 2023 baseline — a meaningful gain. But thermal stress events last summer set certain restoration plots back by weeks. This post summarizes our Year 3 methodology updates, key cover-change findings, and what we are planning for the October internal data review.
excerpt: Three years of transect data reveal cautious recovery — and new thermal stress challenges.

title: Community Reef Monitoring Spring 2026: A Record Volunteer Turnout
content: The first and third Saturdays of April brought out our largest volunteer cohort yet — 34 trained observers across six monitoring sites from Moku Manu to He'eia Kea. Noelani Kahale and Keoni Mahoe coordinated logistics while Lorraine led the safety briefing and transect walk-through. Highlights include three new juvenile Porites colonies documented at Site 4 and reduced algae cover compared with the autumn 2025 survey.
excerpt: Record 34 volunteers completed spring transect surveys across six Kāne'ohe Bay sites.

title: Reef-Safe Sunscreen: Why the Science Matters for Kāne'ohe Bay
content: Oxybenzone and octinoxate have been documented to cause coral bleaching at concentrations as low as 62 parts per trillion. With Kāne'ohe Bay hosting some of the highest recreational snorkeler density in windward Oahu, the cumulative chemical load is real. This post walks through the peer-reviewed evidence and offers a practical guide to mineral-only sunscreen options that are both effective and reef-compatible.
excerpt: A plain-language guide to the chemical evidence and why mineral sunscreen is the only reef-compatible choice.

title: Pacific MPA Policy Brief Update: Submission Deadline October 2026
content: The Pacific Marine Protected Area Policy Brief is entering its final drafting phase ahead of the October 16 submission deadline. Dr. Kamaka outlines the three core recommendations the brief will advance: expanded no-take zones in the northern bay, mandatory reef-safe anchoring for commercial vessels, and a community co-management framework modeled on the successful He'eia Fishpond partnership. Coral Futures staff and partners are invited to submit data citations through the shared Box folder by September 1.
excerpt: Dr. Kamaka previews the three core recommendations in the upcoming Pacific MPA policy brief.

title: How We Use Satellite SST Data to Predict Bleaching Risk in Near Real-Time
content: NOAA CoralWatch Degree Heating Week products give us an eight-day rolling thermal stress indicator. Combined with our in-situ logger network at twelve sites across the bay, we can now flag bleaching-risk conditions within 48 hours of a thermal event. This post describes the data pipeline from NASA GHRSST imagery to our field response protocol — and how David Chun's GIS dashboard has streamlined alert distribution to the monitoring team.
excerpt: Our 48-hour bleaching-risk pipeline links NASA satellite SST to in-situ logger data and field response.

title: Draft: Expanding the Community Monitoring Network to He'eia and Kāne'ohe State Park
content: Early notes on a proposed expansion of our community monitoring network to two additional sites. Draft for review by Dr. Kamaka and Keoni before publication. Pending site-access agreements with DLNR.
excerpt: Work in progress — expansion proposal for two new monitoring sites.

title: A Fisherman's Perspective: Keoni Mahoe on Why Community Monitoring Works
content: Keoni Mahoe has fished Kāne'ohe Bay for over two decades. In this guest post he reflects on watching the reef change — the coral bleaching events of 2015 and 2019, the algae blooms after heavy rains, and what it felt like to join the monitoring program in 2022. 'The scientists needed people who already knew where the fish weren't,' he writes. 'I needed data to show the county what my eyes had been telling me for years.'
excerpt: Keoni Mahoe on two decades of watching the reef change — and why science and traditional knowledge belong together.

title: Draft: Sedimentation Trends 2023–2026 and Coral Cover Recovery
content: Preliminary analysis of sedimentation rate data from our turbidity loggers, cross-referenced with coral cover transect results. Draft — awaiting final QA from Tomás Vega before submission for internal review.
excerpt: Preliminary sedimentation and coral cover analysis — draft pending QA.

title: Active Coral Outplanting at Reef 44: One Year of Monitored Recovery
content: In June 2025 our team outplanted 148 Porites lobata and Montipora capitata fragments onto the degraded substrate of Reef 44 in central Kāne'ohe Bay. One year on, 79 percent of the fragments have survived the first thermal cycle and show measurable growth. This post describes the nursery-to-reef pipeline, the fragment attachment methods we refined over the first season, and why Reef 44 was selected as a priority restoration site despite its mid-bay position and elevated nutrient loading.
excerpt: One-year survivorship data from our 148-fragment outplant at Reef 44 — 79 percent survival through the first thermal cycle.

title: GIS Dashboard Update: Real-Time Transect Coverage Maps Now Live
content: David Chun and I spent the better part of March building out the interactive transect coverage map layer for our internal GIS dashboard. It now shows each monitoring station color-coded by survey recency — green for surveyed within 30 days, amber for 30–90 days, red for overdue. The tool is already changing how we prioritize field days, especially when weather or crew availability forces us to choose between sites. Full walkthrough in this post.
excerpt: Our new interactive survey-coverage layer helps the field team prioritize dive days with real-time station status.

title: Understanding Coral Bleaching: A Plain-Language Primer for Bay Users
content: When water temperatures rise even a degree or two above the summer maximum for four or more weeks, the symbiotic algae that give corals their color — and most of their food — evacuate the coral tissue. What you see as bleaching is a coral under severe stress. This primer, written for snorkelers, paddlers, and anyone who spends time on Kāne'ohe Bay, explains what bleaching looks like underwater, what causes it, and what bay users can do to reduce additional stressors during a bleaching event.
excerpt: A plain-language guide to coral bleaching for snorkelers and bay users — what to look for and how to help.

title: Celebrating Five Years: Coral Futures Institute's 2025 Annual Impact Summary
content: Dr. Kamaka reflects on five years of the Coral Futures Institute: 420 volunteer-hours logged in community monitoring, 23 peer-reviewed publications citing CFI data, three MPA policy submissions, and the establishment of the windward Oahu coral nursery network. This post is a frank accounting of what we set out to do, what we achieved, and where coral reef science still has enormous ground to cover.
excerpt: Dr. Kamaka's candid five-year retrospective on CFI science, partnerships, and what the data still demands of us.

title: Dive Log: KB11 and KB12 Post-Rain Survey, May 2026
content: Field notes from the May 14 dives at stations KB11 and KB12 following the May 9–10 heavy rainfall event. KB11 showed notably depressed salinity (32.1 PSU) consistent with runoff from the He'eia drainage. Visual survey at KB11 recorded pale-white tips on roughly 30 percent of the Acropora colonies in the 1–3 m zone, likely a combination of thermal stress from the preceding warm spell and dilution. KB12, further from the freshwater plume, looked comparatively healthier. Counts and photo documentation attached.
excerpt: Post-rain dive survey at KB11 reveals combined salinity and thermal stress signatures on Acropora colonies.

title: No-Take Zone Science: What the Evidence Says About MPA Effectiveness
content: Ahead of the October policy brief submission, it is worth revisiting the empirical literature on marine protected area effectiveness. A 2023 meta-analysis of 89 MPAs across the Indo-Pacific found that strictly enforced no-take zones recovered fish biomass by an average of 343 percent within 10 years. But enforcement and community buy-in are the critical variables — MPAs designated on paper without active management provide little measurable benefit. This post summarizes the evidence base that underpins our proposed Kāne'ohe Bay expansions.
excerpt: A literature-backed case for strictly enforced no-take zones — and why community co-management is the difference-maker.

title: Volunteer Spotlight: Training New Observers for the Community Monitoring Program
content: Noelani Kahale describes the two-day observer training curriculum she developed for new community monitoring volunteers. Participants learn coral identification for the twelve most common Kāne'ohe Bay species, safe snorkeling protocols, belt transect methodology, and data-entry procedures using the CFI survey app. This cohort of 14 trained observers — including eight who had no prior scientific diving or snorkeling background — demonstrates how accessible structured training can be.
excerpt: Noelani Kahale walks through the two-day training curriculum that turned 14 first-timers into certified reef observers.

title: Draft: Thermal Tolerance Variation Across Kāne'ohe Bay Patch Reef Zones
content: Working draft exploring whether Porites lobata colonies at historically warmer inner-bay sites exhibit measurable thermal tolerance advantages relative to outer-bay conspecifics. Preliminary bleaching threshold comparisons from 2024 and 2025 thermal events suggest a 0.4–0.6 °C differential. Draft — pending genetic cross-referencing with Dr. Whitfield.
excerpt: Draft examining thermal tolerance variation across bay zones — preliminary genetic comparison pending.

title: How to Report a Coral Bleaching Event: A Guide for Bay Users
content: If you see bleached coral on Kāne'ohe Bay, reporting it helps us respond faster and build a richer dataset. This post explains exactly how to submit an observation: take a photo with your phone (GPS metadata on), note your depth estimate and site, and submit via the CFI Reef Monitoring form or by emailing info@coralfuturesinstitute.org. We check reports daily during active thermal stress periods. Every data point from bay users genuinely improves our monitoring coverage.
excerpt: How to submit a reef bleaching observation via the CFI Reef Monitoring form — your photos help us respond faster.

title: Reef Restoration Nursery Update: Kāne'ohe Coral Nursery Q1 2026
content: Our in-water nursery structure at the Kāne'ohe Bay DLNR permit site currently holds 312 coral fragments across eight tree-rack arrays. Survival this quarter was 87 percent — slightly above our 85 percent target. The main losses were during the January 14–17 high-temperature anomaly, which peaked at 29.8 °C at the nursery mooring. Kaipo Spencer and I completed a full cleaning and fragment inspection on February 6; the remaining stock looks robust heading into spring outplanting.
excerpt: 87 percent Q1 survival at our in-water nursery — above target despite a January heat spike.

title: Why Anchoring Damages Reefs — and What Reef-Safe Anchoring Looks Like
content: A single drag from a conventional anchor across a coral head can destroy 50 or more years of coral growth in a single pass. Kāne'ohe Bay sees significant recreational and commercial vessel traffic, and anchor damage is a documented cause of coral cover loss at several of our monitoring stations. This post explains the problem, describes reef-safe anchoring buoy systems already deployed in parts of the bay, and outlines what the MPA policy brief is recommending for commercial vessel requirements.
excerpt: Anchor drag destroys decades of coral growth in seconds — here is what reef-safe anchoring looks like and why we are pushing for mandatory standards.

title: Nutrients and Reef Decline: What the Water Quality Data from Kāne'ohe Bay Shows
content: Tomás Vega, our Lab Manager, walks through three years of nutrient data from the water quality monitoring network. The patterns are striking: elevated ammonium and phosphate at our inner-bay reference sites correlate tightly with rainfall-driven stormwater events, particularly after heavy winter rains. These pulses fuel macroalgae growth that directly competes with recovering coral colonies. Understanding this dynamic is essential context for interpreting the coral cover change data — reef recovery is not just a thermal story.
excerpt: Three years of nutrient time-series data from our monitoring network reveal rainfall-driven stormwater pulses as a key driver of algae competition with recovering corals.
